title: Runtime host capability contract for packaged incidents
description: The packaged app path now truthfully says when Docker-backed replay cannot run in the current app environment, but the capability is still only implied through prose. Add an explicit runtime host capability contract to the incident context so the product can reason about whether replay is unavailable because no bounded pack exists, because Docker is unavailable in the current host, or because replay is available and can be executed. This contract should be returned for seeded incidents and live `nxs_...` incidents, and the incident page should render a concise operator-facing capability state instead of relying only on free-form reasoning strings.
done_when: incident context includes a structured runtime host capability section for INC001, INC002, and fresh nxs incidents
the contract distinguishes at least: no_pack, host_unavailable, and replay_available states
the incident page surfaces the capability state without contradicting replica_summary.reasoning

title: App-triggered replay endpoint for Docker-capable hosts
description: Add an explicit packaged-app replay trigger for bounded incidents so NEXUS can request Docker-backed replay on a Docker-capable host instead of only describing how replay would work. Keep scope intentionally narrow: only the bounded Python/FastAPI flagship packs, only the current replay substrate, and only an operator-triggered execution path. The endpoint should fail closed when the host is not replay-capable, and the incident UI should reflect that state clearly.
done_when: there is an authenticated app endpoint to trigger bounded replay for supported incidents
the endpoint returns a stable contract for success, host_unavailable, and unsupported incidents
the incident UI can request replay and show the returned runtime state without breaking the existing static experience

title: Fresh nxs incident parity with flagship runtime story
description: Fresh `nxs_...` incidents created from `/inputs` should read like a bounded support investigation product, not a fallback-only status seam. Strengthen the live and client fallback paths so fresh incidents inherit the same issue-family mapping, memory posture, runtime capability state, and trace handoff quality as the seeded flagship incidents, while remaining honest about what is inferred versus measured.
done_when: a fresh nxs incident shows bounded runtime capability state, non-generic trace guidance, and memory-backed mitigation language
the fresh incident narrative does not contradict the seeded runtime story
browser verification for the raw log intake path remains green

title: Multi-mitigation REPLICA comparison and decision packet
description: REPLICA currently compresses its result into one winning mitigation. Deepen this into a visible comparison packet that shows baseline, primary mitigation, and runner-up mitigation outcomes when available. Feed that comparison into FORGE, GUARDIAN, and the incident UI so the operator can see why one mitigation won and why another remained only partial or inferior.
done_when: replica_summary returns a stable multi-mitigation comparison structure
FORGE reasoning cites why the chosen mitigation beat the runner-up
GUARDIAN reasoning distinguishes resolved, improved, and runner-up-only evidence
the incident UI shows the comparison without relying on long prose

title: TRACE developer packet v3 and runtime-host demo closure
description: Turn TRACE into a stronger bounded developer handoff for the flagship outages by adding more explicit file-level, owner-level, and suspected divergence cues, then close the loop in demo and docs so the runtime-host model is demonstrated clearly. This slice should leave the repo with one coherent story: seeded incidents, fresh incidents, runtime capability states, replay trigger path, and developer handoff all align.
done_when: TRACE output for INC001 and INC002 contains explicit inspect-here-first guidance with file-level cues
demo.py and the walkthrough docs explain the runtime-host model and replay trigger path accurately
manual verification docs reflect the current packaged-app reality

title: Runtime-host relay contract and internal execution API
description: Add a bounded machine-to-machine contract for REPLICA replay so a packaged NEXUS app can delegate runtime execution to a Docker-capable host instead of assuming replay must happen inside the app container. This slice should only establish the request/response model, internal auth gate, runtime-host endpoint, and platform-status visibility. It should not yet rewire the operator replay button to use the relay.
done_when: there is a stable internal runtime-host replay endpoint protected by a shared token
the endpoint accepts bounded replay inputs and returns a stable execution contract
platform status exposes whether a runtime host relay is configured

title: Packaged-app replay delegation to runtime host
description: Teach `/api/v1/incidents/{id}/replica-replay` to call the runtime-host relay when the packaged app itself is not Docker-capable but a relay host is configured. The incident UI should remain unchanged while the returned runtime-capability state becomes `relay_available` or `relay_executed` instead of `host_unavailable` where appropriate.
done_when: seeded incidents can execute bounded replay from the packaged app through the runtime-host relay
runtime capability truthfully distinguishes direct replay, relay replay, and unavailable states
incident replay responses remain stable for seeded and fresh incidents

title: Docker compose runtime-host profile for local packaged verification
description: Add an optional local runtime-host service or profile to the Docker path so the packaged app can be verified end-to-end on `:7860` with the replay button using a Docker-capable relay. Keep the scope local-only and bounded to the two curated packs.
done_when: docker compose can start a runtime-host relay for local verification
the relay can execute at least INC001 and INC002 bounded packs
the packaged-app smoke path covers relay-backed replay

title: Persist replay evidence on live incidents and surface relay provenance
description: Once relay-backed replay works, persist the latest replay evidence for live incidents so operators do not lose runtime findings on refresh. Surface whether replay came from direct host execution or delegated relay execution.
done_when: live incidents retain the latest replay-backed replica summary after refresh
runtime provenance is visible in the incident payload and UI
fresh incidents stop feeling ephemeral after replay is triggered

title: Repo-aware TRACE ownership cues for flagship packs
description: After relay execution is real, deepen TRACE by making file-level cues more explicit and tying them to pack-aware ownership and replay provenance. Keep this bounded to the curated flagship packs rather than pretending to do universal repository indexing.
done_when: TRACE cites replay-proven failing path plus bounded file/owner cues for INC001 and INC002
developer handoff wording references relay/direct runtime provenance when available
docs and demo narrative reflect the stronger handoff truthfully

title: Replay-aware live overlay recomputation
description: Live `nxs_...` incidents now persist replay-backed REPLICA and TRACE packets, but the broader overlay can still refresh from scaffold-only logic for FORGE, GUARDIAN, memory ranking, and task summaries. Recompute those live overlay sections from the persisted replay packet so the entire incident narrative stays coherent after replay, not just the two investigation cards.
done_when: live incident refresh keeps replay-aware FORGE reasoning and GUARDIAN posture
memory hits are re-ranked against the persisted replay outcome on refresh
task board summaries stay aligned with the persisted REPLICA and TRACE packet

title: Replay history timeline for live incidents
description: Right now the live incident keeps only the latest replay packet. Persist a bounded replay history so operators can see whether repeated runs improved confidence, changed runtime provenance, or flipped the winning mitigation. Keep the scope to a short local incident history, not a full analytics subsystem.
done_when: live incidents retain a bounded list of prior replay packets with timestamps
the incident UI surfaces the latest replay plus prior replay history clearly
relay versus direct runtime provenance is visible per replay entry

title: Bounded repo ownership map from real project metadata
description: TRACE currently uses curated owner and file cues for the flagship packs. Tighten that by reading a bounded ownership map from local project metadata or a checked-in config so the handoff packet references an explicit owner source instead of only hardcoded pack labels. Keep it bounded to the flagship paths rather than promising universal repository indexing.
done_when: TRACE cites an ownership source for flagship files instead of only static labels
the developer handoff packet references both the file path and the bounded owner mapping source
docs explain the bounded nature of the ownership map truthfully

title: Runtime-host platform status and operator visibility
description: Turn the runtime host from an implicit backend detail into an operator-visible product capability. The packaged app should show whether a runtime host is configured, reachable, healthy, and what bounded packs it can execute, so replay availability feels like product truth instead of an implementation accident.
done_when: incident and/or platform views show runtime-host configured vs reachable vs unavailable states clearly
operators can see which flagship bounded packs are relay-capable from the packaged app
runtime capability copy is consistent across seeded and fresh nxs incidents

title: Replay run lifecycle UX for packaged incidents
description: Replay in the packaged app currently behaves like a fire-and-return request. Productize that into a clearer lifecycle with requested, running, completed, and failed states, so operators understand whether they are waiting on a runtime host, reviewing a fresh result, or looking at historical replay evidence.
done_when: the incident UI shows replay requested/running/completed/failed states explicitly
the latest replay history entry reflects lifecycle state transitions rather than only final completion
re-running replay from the packaged app is understandable without reading raw response text

title: Runtime-host trust, audit, and failure explanation packet
description: If runtime-host replay is going to sell as an enterprise feature, the operator needs a trust packet: why replay was allowed, what environment executed it, and why it failed when it fails. Keep the scope bounded to auditability and operator explanation, not a full security subsystem.
done_when: relay-backed replay returns stable operator-facing explanation fields for allowed, denied, and failed executions
incident history or audit surfaces show whether replay came from direct host, relay host, or stayed inferred only
failure cases explain the limiting factor without collapsing to generic errors

title: Replica hypothesis packet v2 for flagship incidents
description: Deepen the bounded reproduction story for INC001 and INC002 by making REPLICA expose the exact environment hypothesis it is validating: triggering conditions, expected failure signature, and the mitigation checkpoints it will test. This is the first step from runtime replay into a more real reproduction agent, while staying tightly scoped to the curated packs.
done_when: REPLICA returns a visible hypothesis packet for INC001 and INC002 with triggering conditions and expected failure signature
fresh nxs incidents mapped to those classes inherit the same bounded hypothesis contract truthfully
operators can explain what REPLICA is trying to prove before the replay even runs

title: Trace-to-code packet with bounded stack and file evidence
description: Move TRACE one step closer to a real debugging assistant by adding a bounded stack-path packet for the flagship incidents: likely function chain, failure boundary, and the runtime clue that pushed TRACE toward that path. Do not attempt universal dynamic debugging yet; keep it specific to the curated outage classes.
done_when: TRACE output includes a bounded stack or call-path narrative for INC001 and INC002
the handoff links the chosen path back to replay evidence instead of only issue-family heuristics
the developer packet feels like a first debugging note rather than only a triage summary

title: Bounded mitigation execution ladder across REPLICA, FORGE, and GUARDIAN
description: Right now mitigation comparison is present, but the product still feels like a single-step recommendation. Deepen that into a bounded execution ladder: preferred mitigation, fallback mitigation, and stop conditions, all derived from the replay comparison and made legible to GUARDIAN and the operator.
done_when: FORGE exposes a primary and bounded fallback mitigation ladder for the flagship outages
GUARDIAN reasoning references the selected ladder and the stop conditions between steps
the incident UI makes it clear what happens if the leading mitigation only improves but does not resolve

title: Flagship bounded debugger packet for one outage class
description: Build the first honest debugging-agent step, but only for one outage class. Start with either INC001 or INC002 and generate a debugger-style packet that shows expected variable/state checkpoints, likely divergence location, and what a human engineer should inspect next in code. This is the safe bridge toward a real debugging agent without pretending to support arbitrary stacks.
done_when: one flagship outage class has a bounded debugger packet that references concrete state checkpoints
the packet is visible in the incident UI and clearly labeled as bounded to the curated pack
docs explain what is implemented versus what remains theoretical
